title: What is a variable?
description: Choose the best definition of a variable in programming.

options.text: A container for storing data values
A type of loop in programming

content.explanation: A variable is a container that stores data values. It can hold different types of data like numbers, text, or boolean values.

title: Array Index
description: In most programming languages, what is the index of the first element in an array?

options.text: It depends on the array size

content.explanation: In most programming languages, arrays use zero-based indexing, meaning the first element is at index 0.

title: String Length
description: Which of the following best describes how to find the length of a string?

options.text: Use a built-in length function or property
Convert it to an array first
Use a loop to iterate through all characters

content.explanation: Most programming languages provide built-in functions or properties (like .length, len(), or .size()) to get the length of a string.

title: Basic Loop Purpose
description: What is the primary purpose of a loop in programming?

options.text: To repeat a block of code multiple times

content.explanation: A loop is used to repeat a block of code multiple times, either for a specific number of iterations or until a certain condition is met.

title: Time Complexity of Binary Search
description: What is the time complexity of binary search in a sorted array?

content.explanation: Binary search has O(log n) time complexity because it eliminates half of the remaining elements in each step.

title: Stack vs Queue
description: What is the main difference between a stack and a queue?

options.text: Stack uses LIFO, Queue uses FIFO
Stack stores numbers, Queue stores strings

content.explanation: Stack follows Last In, First Out (LIFO) principle, while Queue follows First In, First Out (FIFO) principle.

title: Hash Table Collision
description: What happens when two different keys hash to the same index in a hash table?

options.text: A collision resolution strategy is used

content.explanation: When a collision occurs, collision resolution strategies like chaining or open addressing are used to handle multiple keys hashing to the same index.

title: Recursion Base Case
description: What is a base case in recursion?

options.text: The condition that stops the recursion
The most complex part of the algorithm

content.explanation: A base case is the condition that stops the recursive calls, preventing infinite recursion and providing a way to return a result.

title: Dynamic Programming Principle
description: What is the key principle behind dynamic programming?

content.explanation: Dynamic programming relies on optimal substructure (optimal solution contains optimal solutions to subproblems) and overlapping subproblems (same subproblems are solved multiple times).

title: Graph Traversal Difference
description: What is the main difference between DFS and BFS in terms of memory usage?

options.text: DFS uses O(V) space, BFS uses O(V) space
DFS uses O(h) space, BFS uses O(w) space
DFS uses more memory than BFS always
Both use the same amount of memory

content.explanation: DFS uses O(h) space where h is the height of the tree/depth of recursion, while BFS uses O(w) space where w is the maximum width of the tree/graph level.

title: Tree Balancing
description: What is the primary benefit of keeping a binary search tree balanced?

options.text: Maintains O(log n) time complexity for operations

content.explanation: A balanced BST ensures that the height remains O(log n), which maintains O(log n) time complexity for search, insert, and delete operations.

title: Space-Time Tradeoff
description: In algorithm design, what does 'space-time tradeoff' typically refer to?

options.text: Using more time to save space
Using more space to save time
Trading off between memory usage and execution time
The physical space needed to run algorithms

content.explanation: Space-time tradeoff refers to the balance between memory usage and execution time - often you can make an algorithm faster by using more memory, or use less memory but take more time.

title: Simple Array Access
description: If you have an array arr = [10, 20, 30, 40], what is arr[2]?

content.explanation: Array indexing starts at 0, so arr[2] refers to the third element, which is 30.

title: Boolean Values
description: Which of the following are valid boolean values?

content.explanation: Boolean values are true and false. While some languages may convert 1/0 to boolean, the actual boolean values are true and false.

title: Simple Condition
description: What does an if statement do?

options.text: Executes code only if a condition is true

content.explanation: An if statement executes a block of code only when the specified condition evaluates to true.

title: String Concatenation
description: What does string concatenation mean?

options.text: Joining two or more strings together
Finding a character in a string

content.explanation: String concatenation is the process of joining two or more strings together to form a single string.

title: Array Size
description: How do you typically find the number of elements in an array?

content.explanation: Most programming languages provide a length property or function to get the number of elements in an array.

title: Basic Function Purpose
description: What is the main purpose of functions in programming?

options.text: To organize code into reusable blocks

content.explanation: Functions allow you to organize code into reusable blocks that can be called multiple times with different inputs.

title: What is an Algorithm?
description: What best describes an algorithm?

options.text: A step-by-step procedure to solve a problem

content.explanation: An algorithm is a finite sequence of well-defined instructions to solve a specific problem or perform a computation.

title: Simple Linear Search
description: In linear search, how do you find an element in an array?

options.text: Check each element one by one from start to end
Jump to the middle and search from there
Search from the end to the beginning only

content.explanation: Linear search examines each element in the array sequentially from the beginning until the target element is found or the end is reached.

title: Basic Time Complexity
description: What does O(1) time complexity mean?

options.text: The operation takes constant time regardless of input size

content.explanation: O(1) means constant time complexity - the operation takes the same amount of time regardless of the input size.

title: What is a Stack?
description: Which analogy best describes a stack data structure?

content.explanation: A stack is like a pile of plates - you can only add or remove items from the top (Last In, First Out - LIFO).

title: Simple Queue Concept
description: Which analogy best describes a queue data structure?

options.text: A line of people waiting for service

content.explanation: A queue is like a line of people waiting - the first person in line is the first to be served (First In, First Out - FIFO).

title: Bubble Sort Complexity
description: What is the worst-case time complexity of bubble sort?

content.explanation: Bubble sort has O(n²) worst-case time complexity because it compares every pair of elements, resulting in nested loops.

title: Linked List Insertion
description: What is the time complexity of inserting an element at the beginning of a linked list?

content.explanation: Inserting at the beginning of a linked list is O(1) because you only need to update the head pointer and the new node's next pointer.

title: Binary Tree Height
description: What is the maximum height of a binary tree with n nodes?

content.explanation: The maximum height occurs when the tree is completely unbalanced (like a linked list), giving a height of n-1.

title: Queue Implementation
description: Which data structure is commonly used to implement a queue efficiently?

content.explanation: A queue is efficiently implemented using an array with two pointers (front and rear) or a linked list.

title: Hash Function Properties
description: What is a desirable property of a good hash function?

options.text: Distributes keys uniformly across the hash table

content.explanation: A good hash function should distribute keys uniformly across the hash table to minimize collisions and ensure good performance.

title: Array vs Linked List Access
description: What is the main advantage of arrays over linked lists for accessing elements?

content.explanation: Arrays provide O(1) random access to elements using indices, while linked lists require O(n) traversal to reach a specific position.

title: Depth-First Search
description: Which data structure is typically used to implement depth-first search (DFS)?

content.explanation: DFS uses a stack (either explicitly or implicitly through recursion) to keep track of vertices to visit.

title: Binary Search Tree Property
description: What property must a binary search tree maintain?

options.text: All nodes have exactly two children
Left subtree values < root < right subtree values
All leaves are at the same level

content.explanation: A BST maintains the property that all values in the left subtree are less than the root, and all values in the right subtree are greater than the root.

title: Merge Sort Complexity
description: What is the time complexity of merge sort in all cases?

content.explanation: Merge sort consistently has O(n log n) time complexity in best, average, and worst cases due to its divide-and-conquer approach.

title: String Pattern Matching
description: What is the time complexity of the naive string pattern matching algorithm?

content.explanation: The naive pattern matching algorithm has O(m*n) complexity where m is pattern length and n is text length, as it may check every position.

title: Breadth-First Search
description: Which data structure is typically used to implement breadth-first search (BFS)?

content.explanation: BFS uses a queue to process vertices level by level, ensuring that all vertices at distance k are processed before vertices at distance k+1.

title: Recursive Factorial
description: What is the space complexity of a recursive factorial function?

content.explanation: Recursive factorial has O(n) space complexity due to the call stack storing n function calls.

title: Heap Property
description: In a max heap, what property must be maintained?

content.explanation: In a max heap, every parent node must have a value greater than or equal to its children.

title: AVL Tree Rotation
description: When is a double rotation needed in an AVL tree?

options.text: When the imbalance forms a zig-zag pattern
When inserting into an empty tree

content.explanation: A double rotation (left-right or right-left) is needed when the imbalance forms a zig-zag pattern, where single rotation would not fix the imbalance.

title: Dijkstra's Algorithm Complexity
description: What is the time complexity of Dijkstra's algorithm using a binary heap?

content.explanation: Using a binary heap, Dijkstra's algorithm has O(E log V) complexity, where E is the number of edges and V is the number of vertices.

title: Red-Black Tree Properties
description: How many properties must a red-black tree satisfy?

content.explanation: A red-black tree must satisfy 5 properties: root is black, leaves are black, red nodes have black children, all paths have same black height, and new insertions are red.

title: Fibonacci Dynamic Programming
description: What is the time complexity of computing Fibonacci numbers using dynamic programming?

content.explanation: Dynamic programming reduces Fibonacci computation from O(2^n) to O(n) by storing previously computed values and avoiding redundant calculations.

title: Topological Sort Requirement
description: What type of graph is required for topological sorting?

content.explanation: Topological sorting is only possible on directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) because cycles would create impossible ordering dependencies.

title: Quick Sort Worst Case
description: When does Quick Sort perform worst with O(n²) complexity?

options.text: When the array is already sorted
When the array is randomly shuffled
When the array has duplicate elements

content.explanation: Quick Sort performs worst when the pivot is always the smallest or largest element, which happens with sorted arrays using simple pivot selection.

title: Knapsack Problem Type
description: What type of algorithm optimization problem is the 0/1 Knapsack problem?

content.explanation: The 0/1 Knapsack problem is solved optimally using dynamic programming due to its optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems.

title: B-Tree Properties
description: What is the minimum number of keys in a non-root node of a B-tree of order m?

content.explanation: In a B-tree of order m, each non-root node must have at least ⌈m/2⌉ - 1 keys to maintain the B-tree properties.

title: Minimum Spanning Tree
description: Which algorithm is NOT used to find minimum spanning trees?

content.explanation: Dijkstra's algorithm finds shortest paths from a source vertex, not minimum spanning trees. Kruskal's, Prim's, and Borůvka's algorithms find MSTs.

title: Heap Sort Stability
description: Is Heap Sort a stable sorting algorithm?

options.text: Yes, it always maintains relative order
No, it can change relative order of equal elements
It depends on the heap implementation

content.explanation: Heap Sort is not stable because the heap operations can change the relative order of equal elements during the sorting process.

title: Longest Common Subsequence
description: What is the time complexity of finding the LCS of two strings of length m and n using dynamic programming?

content.explanation: The LCS dynamic programming solution uses a 2D table of size m×n, resulting in O(m*n) time complexity.

title: Trie Space Complexity
description: What is the worst-case space complexity of a Trie storing n strings of maximum length m?

content.explanation: In the worst case, a Trie storing n strings of length m can have O(n*m) nodes when there's minimal prefix sharing between strings.

title: Union-Find Optimization
description: What optimization technique improves Union-Find operations to nearly O(1) amortized time?

options.text: Path compression and union by rank

content.explanation: Combining path compression with union by rank gives Union-Find operations O(α(n)) amortized time, where α is the inverse Ackermann function.

title: NP-Complete Problem
description: Which of the following is an NP-Complete problem?

content.explanation: The Traveling Salesman Problem is NP-Complete, meaning no known polynomial-time algorithm exists to solve it optimally.

title: Skip List Operations
description: What is the expected time complexity of search operations in a Skip List?

content.explanation: Skip Lists provide O(log n) expected time complexity for search, insert, and delete operations through their probabilistic layered structure.
